elements.sent-2411-001: We study the existence of algebras of hypercyclic vectors for weighted backward shifts on sequence spaces of directed trees with the coordinatewise product.
elements.sent-2411-002: When $V$ is a rooted directed tree, we show the set of hypercyclic vectors of any backward weighted shift operator on the space $c_0(V)$ or $\ell^1(V)$ is algebrable whenever it is not empty.
elements.sent-2411-003: We provide necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of these structures on $\ell^p(V), 1<p<+\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Examples of hypercyclic operators not having a hypercyclic algebra are found.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also study the existence of mixing and non-mixing backward weighted shift operators on any rooted directed tree, with or without hypercyclic algebras.
elements.sent-2411-006: The case of unrooted trees is also studied.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the field of Linear Dynamics, one studies various dynamical properties satisfied by a linear map $T:X\to X$ acting on a linear space $X$.
elements.sent-2411-008: One of the main notion in the theory is that of hypercyclicity, that is, the presence in $X$ of a dense orbit under the action of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Let $X$ be a complex separable Banach space and $T:X\to X$ be a bounded linear operator.
elements.sent-2411-010: We say that $T$ is hypercyclic when there is a vector $x\in X$ whose orbit $\Orb(x,T):=\{T^nx:\, n\in\NN_0\}$ is dense in $X$, where $\NN_0:=\NN\cup\{0\}$.
elements.sent-2411-011: Such a vector is called a hypercyclic vector for the operator $T$, which in this case is said to be a hypercyclic operator.
elements.sent-2411-012: The set of hypercyclic vectors for an operator $T$ will be denoted by $HC(T)$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Due to the importance of this topic, it is not a huge surprise that there is a big interest in the study of the structure of the set of hypercyclic vectors for a given hypercyclic operator.
elements.sent-2411-014: It is known from \cite[Theorem 9.20]{GottHedl that $HC(T)$ is always either empty or residual, and that in some cases it can be spaceable (as shown by Bernal-Gonz\'alez and Montes-Rodr\'iguez in \cite{bernal1995 ) and even algebrable (as shown by B\`es and Papathanasiou in \cite{bes2020algebrable ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: One of the most studied classes of operators in Linear Dynamics is that of backward shifts.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is of course not a surprise: shift operators are simple to define; they are highly flexible for examples and counter-examples; their orbits are easy to calculate; they can present the most wild behavior; in many cases, other classes of operators can be identified as shifts in some sense.
elements.sent-2411-017: Thus, it is natural to study the aforementioned properties in this context, what has been done on rather general Fréchet sequence spaces.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, when it comes to weighted backward shifts operators $B_w$ acting on spaces like $c_0(\NN)$ or $\ell^p(\NN), 1\leq p<+\infty$, (or even $c_0(\ZZ)$ and $\ell^p(\ZZ)$), their hypercyclicity and the spaceability of $HC(B_w)$ are completely characterized (see \cite{salas , \cite[Section 4.1]{karlbook and \cite{menet2014subspaces ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The search for algebras of hypercyclic vector is also of great interest in the literature (see for example \cite{bes2018algebra , \cite{bayart2019algebra and \cite{besernst2020algebra ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In particular, sufficient conditions for the algebrability of $HC(B_w)$ were obtained in \cite{karl-falco and \cite{BCP .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These last results deal with ``classical'' sequences spaces.
elements.sent-2411-022: In the recent work \cite{Karl1 by K.-G.. Grosse-Erdmann and D.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Papathanasiou, among other things, they characterize hypercyclic and mixing weighted backward shifts acting on sequence spaces of a directed tree.
elements.sent-2411-024: Continuing this trend, in this paper we aim to study hypercyclic algebras in this context.
elements.sent-2411-025: More generally, we are interested in the algebrability of $HC(T)$ when $T$ is a weighted backward shift acting on the sequence space of a tree, which is a Banach algebra when endowed with the coordinatewise product.
elements.sent-2411-026: In Section \ref{sec:prel , we present the main definitions and key results that will be used throughout this work.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Section \ref{sec:rooted addresses the case of rooted directed trees: we characterize weighted backward shift operators on $\ell^p$-spaces, $1 \leq p < +\infty$, and $c_0$-spaces of directed trees that have hypercyclic algebras, and we provide some examples and counterexamples in the case of $\ell^p$-spaces with $1<p<+\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In Section \ref{sec:unrooted , we turn our attention to the unrooted case, offering sufficient conditions for weighted backward shifts to support hypercyclic algebras.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Section \ref{sec:existence then explores the existence of backward shifts that support a hypercyclic algebra on a given $\ell^p$-space, $1 \leq p < +\infty$, or $c_0$-space of a tree.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:conc , we discuss some challenges, pose open problems, and provide perspectives for future research on this topic.}
elements.sent-2411-031: A directed tree is a connected directed graph $(A,E)$, where $A$ is a countable set whose elements are called vertices, and $E\subset\{(a,b)\in A\times A:\, a\neq b\}$ represents the set of edges.
elements.sent-2411-032: This graph possesses the following properties: it has no cycles, meaning there are no paths that lead back to a vertex already visited, and each vertex $b \in A$ has at most one parent.
elements.sent-2411-033: The parent of a vertex $b \in A$, denoted by $\Par(b)$, is a vertex $a \in A$ for which $(a,b) \in E$.
elements.sent-2411-035: There is at most one vertex without a parent, called root, and denoted simply by $\root$.
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, a rooted tree is a tree that has a root.
elements.sent-2411-037: For more details, we refer to \cite{Karl1,Jablonski, serre2002trees and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The parent-child relationship can also be expressed as $b$ being a child of $a$ whenever $a$ is the parent of $b$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The set $\Chi(a)$ stands for the set of children of a vertex $a$.
elements.sent-2411-040: We do not make any restrictions on the number of children a vertex can have (notice that they are at most countable by definition).
elements.sent-2411-041: However, we consider only leafless trees, meaning every vertex must have at least one child.
elements.sent-2411-042: This assumption is necessary for the existence of hypercyclic shifts operators, see \cite[Remark 4.1]{Karl1 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Weighted backward shift operators are defined on $\KK^A$ (the space of maps from $A$ into $\KK$, where $\KK$ is either the real field $\RR$ or the complex field $\CC$) by, for any $f\in\KK^A$ and $v\in A$, \begin{equation}(B_{\lambda}f)(v)=\sum_{u\in\Chi(v)}\lambda_u f(u),\end{equation} where $\lambda=(\lambda_u)_{u\in A}$ is a family of non-zero scalars called weight.
elements.sent-2411-044: For every integer $n\geq1$, the $n$-th iteration of a weighted backward shift on a directed tree can be computed as \begin{equation}(B_{\lambda}^{n}f)(v)=\sum_{u\in\Chi^n(v)}\lambda(v\to u)f(u),\end{equation} where $\lambda(v\to u):=\prod_{k=0 ^{n-1}\lambda_{\Par^{k (u)}$, $\Chi^0(v):=\{v\}$ and \begin{equation}\Chi^n(v):=\bigcup_{u\in \Chi^{n-1}(v)}\Chi(u).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-440: In this paper, we have explored the existence of hypercyclic algebras for backward shifts on sequence spaces of directed trees, equipped with the coordinatewise product.
elements.sent-2411-441: Our investigation has led to several key findings, including characterizations for shifts on rooted trees.
elements.sent-2411-442: The study of convolution products is left open for exploration and can lead to interesting results on $\ell^1$-spaces of a tree.
